1: Type: YHWH speaks this law on Mount Sinai — the foundational covenant location. The land-sabbath law that follows has a direct Christological fulfillment. Matthew 11:28–30: “Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest... my yoke is easy, and my burden is light.” The rest YHWH builds into the agricultural year through shemittah and Jubilee is a shadow of the rest Christ offers to all who come to him.
2: Type: “The land shall observe a sabbath to YHWH.” The sabbath rest of the land is a creational principle applied to agricultural space and time. Hebrews 4:9–11: “So then, there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God... Let us therefore strive to enter that rest.” The land’s sabbath year is one layer in a cascade of sabbath-rest typologies that climaxes in the eschatological rest Christ opens.
3: Shadow: Six years of cultivation are permitted — sowing, pruning, gathering. The six-plus-one pattern of the week is extended to the year: six years of labor, one of rest. John 9:4–5: Jesus says, “We must work the works of him who sent me while it is day; night is coming, when no one can work. As long as I am in the world, I am the light of the world.” The rhythm of labor followed by cessation anticipates the completed work of Christ that makes rest possible.
4: Type: The seventh year is a shabbat shabbaton — a sabbath of complete rest for the land. The superlative construction signals the highest sabbath observance. John 19:30: from the cross, Christ declares “It is finished” (tetelestai) — the work of atonement is complete. The land’s total rest in the seventh year is a typological pointer to the completed work of Christ, the one whose “it is finished” inaugurates the rest that all the sabbath laws anticipated.
5: Shadow: The volunteer crop (sephiach) and unpruned grapes may not be commercially harvested — they belong to all freely. The seventh-year produce that arises without human effort and is freely available to all foreshadows the grace of the gospel. Isaiah 55:1: “Come, everyone who thirsts, come to the waters; and he who has no money, come, buy and eat!” Revelation 22:17: “let the one who desires take the water of life without price.” The free bounty of the sabbath year is a seed of grace’s free provision.
6: Shadow: The seventh-year produce is for everyone equally — owner, servant, hired hand, sojourner, cattle, and wild animals. This radical social leveling of the sabbath year anticipates the gospel’s equal accessibility. Galatians 3:28: in Christ “there is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is no male and female.” The boundary distinctions of normal social life are suspended in the Jubilee’s free bounty, just as they are transcended in Christ’s new community.
7: Revelation of God: Wild animals share in the seventh-year produce. The inclusion of non-human creation in the rest and bounty of the sabbath year reveals YHWH’s care for all creation. Romans 8:21–22: “the creation itself will be set free from its bondage to corruption and obtain the freedom of the glory of the children of God.” The liberation Christ brings is not only for humans but for the whole creation that groans, awaiting the Jubilee of the new creation.
8: Type: Seven weeks of years (49 years) lead to the Jubilee, mirroring the seven weeks of days leading to Pentecost. Acts 2:1–4: the Day of Pentecost (50 days after Passover/First Fruits) brings the outpouring of the Spirit — the fulfillment of the “jubilee” structure. The 50th-year/50th-day pattern is not coincidental: both Pentecost and Jubilee are eschatological release celebrations. The Spirit poured out at Pentecost is the down payment of the eschatological Jubilee Christ inaugurates.
9: Type: The Jubilee is proclaimed by a ram’s horn (shofar teru'ah) on the Day of Atonement. The shofar blast is the sound of divine proclamation and eschatological announcement. 1 Thessalonians 4:16: at Christ’s return “the Lord himself will descend from heaven with a cry of command, with the voice of an archangel, and with the sound of the trumpet of God.” 1 Corinthians 15:52: “the trumpet will sound, and the dead will be raised imperishable.” The Jubilee shofar on Atonement Day foreshadows the trumpet that announces the final liberation at Christ’s return.
10: Direct type: “Proclaim liberty (deror) throughout the land to all its inhabitants.” This is the most direct Jubilee-Christ connection in the NT. Luke 4:16–21: Jesus reads Isaiah 61:1–2 in the Nazareth synagogue (“to proclaim liberty to the captives” — the same deror-vocabulary) and declares, “Today this Scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing.” Jesus claims to be the personal inauguration of the Jubilee — the year of the Lord’s favor made flesh.
11: Direct type: The Jubilee year is itself a sabbatical year — no sowing, no harvest of aftergrowth, no grape-gathering. The Jubilee intensifies and embodies all the sabbath principles. Christ’s declaration of “the year of the Lord’s favor” (Luke 4:19) frames his entire ministry as the Jubilee year: healings restore what was lost, sins are forgiven (debts canceled), the poor receive good news. His ministry is the enacted Jubilee.
12: Type: The Jubilee is “holy” (qodesh): its produce is eaten directly, not harvested commercially. Holiness removes from ordinary commercial exchange. 1 Corinthians 6:20: “you were bought with a price. So glorify God in your body.” The Christian’s status before God is the opposite of commercial: not slaves sold in the market but people redeemed at infinite cost who now belong wholly to God, outside the economy of human exchange.
13: Type: In the Jubilee, each person returns to their original property (ahuzzah). The restoration to ancestral inheritance is a central Jubilee action. 1 Peter 1:3–4: “we have been born again to a living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead, to an inheritance that is imperishable, undefiled, and unfading, kept in heaven for you.” The return to ancestral land in Jubilee is a shadow of the eschatological inheritance restored to God’s people through Christ’s resurrection.
14: Theme: “You shall not wrong one another.” The ethical command embedded in the economic law grounds the Jubilee’s justice provisions in the relationship between neighbors. Leviticus 19:18 (love your neighbor as yourself) is the summary principle. Jesus cites this as the second great commandment (Matt 22:39). The anti-exploitation laws of the Jubilee are applications of the love-neighbor principle — Christ names the center what Leviticus builds toward.
15: Shadow: The pricing formula (sell by years remaining, not land itself) treats time as the commodity, not land. This reflects a theology of stewardship over ownership. Luke 16:12: “if you have not been faithful in that which is another’s, who will give you that which is your own?” The Jubilee pricing model treats all land as ultimately YHWH’s (Leviticus 25:23), making every transaction a stewardship of someone else’s property — the posture Christ calls his disciples to toward all possessions.
16: Shadow: Many years remaining = higher price; few years remaining = lower price. The inverse relationship of time-remaining to price models the transience of earthly possession. Matthew 6:19–20: “Do not lay up for yourselves treasures on earth, where moth and rust destroy... but lay up for yourselves treasures in heaven.” Earthly property is time-limited; heavenly treasure is not subject to the Jubilee reduction.
17: Revelation of God: “You shall not wrong one another, but you shall fear your God, for I am YHWH your God.” The divine name as the ground of economic ethics appears here as in the Decalogue: YHWH’s identity creates the moral obligation. Colossians 3:23–24: “Whatever you do, work heartily, as for the Lord and not for men, knowing that from the Lord you will receive the inheritance as your reward.” The fear of the Lord that anchors Levitical economic law is the same motivation Paul gives for Christian economic conduct.
18: Type: Obedience to YHWH’s statutes brings security in the land. The covenant-obedience-security sequence is consistently developed in Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy. Christ is the one who perfectly fulfills this covenant: Matthew 3:17: “This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased.” His perfect obedience secures the inheritance for all who are united to him — the security the land-sabbath promised is now secured in the one who obeyed completely.
19: Type: “The land will yield its fruit, and you will eat your fill and dwell in it securely.” The promised fertility and security of the land anticipates the eschatological abundance of the new creation. Revelation 22:2: the tree of life bears fruit every month and its leaves heal the nations — a creation restored beyond the Edenic original. The Levitical promise of land-fertility and safety is a thumbnail of the new Jerusalem’s inexhaustible provision.
20: Theme: The anticipated objection — “What shall we eat in the seventh year?” — is a question of faith in YHWH’s provision. The Jubilee requires trust that goes beyond visible means. Matthew 6:25–26: “Do not be anxious about your life, what you will eat or what you will drink... Look at the birds of the air: they neither sow nor reap nor gather into barns, and yet your heavenly Father feeds them.” The sabbath-year question is the original form of the anxiety Jesus addresses.
21: Type: YHWH promises to command his blessing on the sixth year for a triple-yield harvest. The miraculous provision before the rest mirrors the manna-double-portion on the sixth day (Ex Leviticus 16:22-26). 2 Corinthians 9:8: “God is able to make all grace abound to you, so that having all sufficiency in all things at all times, you may abound in every good work.” The principle of divine provision exceeding normal limits is the Jubilee logic applied to grace.
22: Shadow: Eating the old crop into the eighth year while the new crop is growing models patience and trust in overlapping provisions. Philippians 4:19: “my God will supply every need of yours according to his riches in glory in Christ Jesus.” The promise that last year’s provision stretches to cover the gap before new growth arrives models the sufficiency of Christ’s resources for every season of the believer’s life.
23: Direct type: “The land shall not be sold in perpetuity, for the land is mine (ki li ha-aretz). For you are strangers and sojourners with me.” YHWH’s ownership of everything is the theological ground of the Jubilee. 1 Corinthians 6:19–20: “you are not your own, for you were bought with a price.” The Christian’s status is the NT equivalent of the Israelite’s: not owners but stewards of what belongs to God, and therefore free from the anxiety of maintaining what was never theirs to keep.
24: Type: The right of redemption (ge'ullah) throughout the land guarantees that alienated property can always return. The go'el system makes redemption structurally available. Christ is the ultimate kinsman-redeemer. 1 Peter 1:18–19: “you were ransomed from the futile ways inherited from your forefathers, not with perishable things such as silver or gold, but with the precious blood of Christ, like that of a lamb without blemish or spot.” The go'el’s right to pay redemption is fulfilled in Christ who pays what we cannot pay.
25: Type: “His nearest redeemer (go'alo ha-qarov) shall come and redeem what his brother has sold.” The kinsman-redeemer must be qarov — near, close. Christ as redeemer takes on human nature to become near. Hebrews 2:14–17: “Since therefore the children share in flesh and blood, he himself likewise partook of the same things... Therefore he had to be made like his brothers in every respect, so that he might become a merciful and faithful high priest.” Incarnation is the act of becoming the nearest kinsman.
26: Type: Self-redemption when resources allow is also valid. Christ as the one who had infinite resources chose to use them for redemption. John 10:17–18: “No one takes my life from me, but I lay it down of my own accord. I have authority to lay it down, and I have authority to take it up again.” His redemption is not compelled by circumstance but chosen from abundance — not a desperate kinsman with just enough, but one with all authority who freely pays the redemption price.
27: Shadow: The refund calculation (years remaining × annual yield) when self-redeeming shows that redemption is a precise accounting, not a rough settlement. Romans 5:8: “while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.” The atonement is not an approximate or partial payment but a precise and complete one: “it is finished” (John 19:30). The Jubilee redemption arithmetic points to the completeness of Christ’s atoning work.
28: Type: If redemption is not possible, the land returns automatically at Jubilee — the absolute backstop. No family can be permanently dispossessed. Romans 8:38–39: “neither death nor life, nor angels nor rulers, nor things present nor things to come, nor powers, nor height nor depth, nor anything else in all creation, will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord.” The Jubilee’s guaranteed return is a shadow of the security of the believer’s inheritance in Christ.
29: Shadow: The one-year redemption window for walled-city houses acknowledges that urban property operates differently from tribal land. The flexibility of the law to distinguish context mirrors the gospel’s adaptation to different human situations. 1 Corinthians 9:19–22: Paul becomes “all things to all people” — the same gospel applied with contextual sensitivity to different circumstances, as the Jubilee law applies its principles with contextual sensitivity to different property types.
30: Shadow: Urban houses in walled cities that are not redeemed within the year become permanent property of the buyer — the Jubilee does not apply. The limit of the Jubilee’s reach acknowledges the complexity of urban economic life. Luke 18:29–30: those who leave houses for the kingdom receive “many times more in this time, and in the age to come eternal life.” The things the Jubilee cannot restore in this age, the age to come restores abundantly.
31: Shadow: Unwalled village houses return at Jubilee — treated as rural land. The distinction between permanent city and impermanent village mirrors the NT distinction between the present age and the age to come. Hebrews 13:14: “here we have no lasting city, but we seek the city that is to come.” The Christian posture toward earthly possessions is like the unwalled-village house: held loosely, knowing that Jubilee-restoration is coming.
32: Type: Levitical city houses are always redeemable because the Levites have no tribal land allotment. The Levites are protected from permanent dispossession by a special provision. Christ as the fulfillment of the Levitical priesthood is the special provision that ensures his priestly people are never permanently lost. John 10:28–29: “I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand.”
33: Shadow: Levitical houses always return at Jubilee — nothing can permanently separate the Levites from their cities. The guaranteed return of the priestly tribe to their communities anticipates the guarantee of the priestly people’s return to their inheritance. 1 Peter 2:9: all believers are “a royal priesthood” whose inheritance is guaranteed by Christ. The special protection for Levites becomes, in Christ, the universal protection of all who serve God.
34: Revelation of God: Levitical pasture land is completely inalienable — it can never be sold. The absolute protection of what is needed for the priestly service reveals YHWH’s commitment to maintaining the infrastructure of worship. Hebrews 7:24: Christ “holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever.” What the inalienable Levitical pastureland protects in type, Christ’s eternal priesthood secures in reality.
35: Type: “If your brother becomes poor and cannot maintain himself with you, you shall support him... and he shall live with you.” The command to let the destitute brother “live with you” (vechai 'immakh) is the positive social corollary of the Jubilee. John 14:3: “I will come again and will take you to myself, that where I am you may be also.” The command to let the impoverished brother live alongside you foreshadows Christ’s bringing his people to dwell with him permanently.
36: Type: No interest (neshekh) on loans to a brother — the prohibition of exploitative gain from another’s desperation. 2 Corinthians 8:9: “though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich.” Christ’s incarnation is the anti-usury principle applied at cosmic scale: not taking advantage of human need but entering human poverty to enrich it. The interest-prohibition is a shadow of grace’s anti-exploitation logic.
37: Theme: No interest on money, no profit on food given to a brother in need. The two categories (money + food) cover all basic economic relationships. Acts 2:44–45: the early Jerusalem church practiced a voluntary version of this: “all who believed were together and had all things in common. And they were selling their possessions and belongings and distributing the proceeds to all, as any had need.” The Pentecost community enacted the Jubilee’s anti-exploitation economics as a fruit of the Spirit’s outpouring.
38: Type: “I am YHWH your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt to give you the land of Canaan, and to be your God.” The Exodus covenant-identity is the ground of economic ethics. Colossians 1:13–14: “He has delivered us from the domain of darkness and transferred us to the kingdom of his beloved Son, in whom we have redemption, the forgiveness of sins.” The new Exodus accomplished by Christ creates the same identity-based ethical obligation — the redeemed community lives by the economics of the Redeemer.
39: Type: An impoverished Israelite who sells himself cannot be treated as a slave but must be treated as a hired worker and sojourner. The refusal to commodify the Israelite person is grounded in covenant identity. Philemon 16: Paul asks Philemon to receive the formerly enslaved Onesimus “no longer as a bondservant but better than a bondservant, as a dear brother” — the gospel transforms the master-slave relationship by the same logic: the one bought with Christ’s blood cannot be treated as mere property.
40: Shadow: The debt-worker serves until Jubilee, then goes free with his children. Family restoration is part of the Jubilee release — not just personal freedom but communal reintegration. Ephesians 2:19: “you are no longer strangers and aliens, but you are fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God.” Christ’s Jubilee-liberation restores not only individuals but their belonging to community — the family goes free together.
41: Type: He returns to his family and to the possession of his fathers. The double restoration (family + land) is the Jubilee’s social vision of wholeness. Romans 8:23: we “wait eagerly for adoption as sons, the redemption of our bodies.” The bodily resurrection and the restoration of creation are the eschatological equivalents of the Jubilee’s physical return: full embodied restoration to the family of God and the inheritance of the new creation.
42: Direct type: “For they are my servants (avadai) whom I brought out of the land of Egypt; they shall not be sold as slaves.” YHWH’s ownership claim — they are already his servants because of the Exodus — is the ground of their freedom from other masters. 1 Corinthians 7:22–23: “he who was called in the Lord as a bondservant is a freedman of the Lord. Likewise he who was free when called is a bondservant of Christ. You were bought with a price; do not become bondservants of men.” The NT exactly reapplies the Jubilee logic to the believer’s new covenant identity.
43: Type: “You shall not rule over him ruthlessly (be-farekhk), but shall fear your God.” Parekhk is the crushing labor of Egypt (Ex Leviticus 1:13-14). The prohibition is: do not be what Egypt was to Israel. Matthew 11:28–30: “Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest... my yoke is easy, and my burden is light.” Christ’s “easy yoke” is the explicit antithesis of the Egyptian parekhk — he has come to replace crushing labor with rest.
44: Shadow: Foreign slaves may be held permanently. This provision, jarring in context, reflects the limit of the covenant community’s reach in the old covenant era. The gospel dissolves this limit. Galatians 3:28: in Christ “there is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free.” Colossians 3:11: “here there is not Greek and Jew, circumcised and uncircumcised, barbarian, Scythian, slave, free; but Christ is all, and in all.” The ethnic and social distinctions that structured the old covenant’s limited reach are overcome in Christ.
45: Shadow: Resident aliens and their locally-born descendants could also be enslaved. This reflects the old covenant’s limited application of covenant protections to non-Israelites. Ephesians 2:11–13: Paul addresses those who were “strangers to the covenants of promise, having no hope and without God in the world” but who have now been “brought near by the blood of Christ.” The gospel extends the Jubilee’s protections to those who were once outside its scope.
46: Shadow: Foreign slaves could be bequeathed as permanent property. The institution persists within the old covenant economy. Philemon 15–16: Paul addresses exactly this situation — an enslaved person now in Christ — and says to receive him “as a dear brother... both in the flesh and in the Lord.” The gospel does not immediately overturn social structures but transforms the relationships within them from the inside, a different but more radical movement than the Jubilee’s external reset.
47: Type: An Israelite who becomes enslaved to a wealthy resident alien faces the most complex redemption case. The reversal of normal social hierarchy (Israelite serving a foreigner) mirrors the incarnation’s logic. Philippians 2:7–8: Christ “emptied himself, by taking the form of a servant... he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death.” The go’el must reach into a foreign master’s household to redeem his kin; Christ enters the domain of sin and death to redeem his people.
48: Type: Even when sold to a foreigner, the kinsman-redeemer (go'el) has the right to redeem. Romans 8:31–32: “If God is for us, who can be against us? He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all, how will he not also with him graciously give us all things?” The go'el’s right to redemption regardless of who holds his kinsman mirrors God’s unassailable right to redeem his people from any power that holds them.
49: Type: The hierarchy of redeemers (brother, uncle, cousin, clansman, self) creates concentric circles of responsibility. Hebrews 2:11–12: “For he who sanctifies and those who are sanctified all have one source. That is why he is not ashamed to call them brothers, saying, ‘I will tell of your name to my brothers.’” Christ as kinsman-redeemer establishes his kinship by sharing human nature — making himself the nearest possible go'el.
50: Shadow: The proportional redemption calculation (years remaining × annual value) treats the person’s labor as having a precise time-value. 1 Corinthians 6:20: “you were bought with a price.” The price paid for believers is not an approximate or partial redemption but a complete purchase. Christ’s blood is not a down payment on a long-term installment plan but a once-for-all price that fully satisfies the claim (Heb 9:12).
51: Shadow: If many years remain, a large buyback price is required. The greater the time of service remaining, the more costly the redemption. Romans 5:6–8: “while we were still weak... while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.” The costliness of redemption is not reduced by the condition of the one redeemed. Christ pays the full price regardless of how deep in slavery his people are when he reaches them.
52: Shadow: Few years remaining = smaller redemption price. Even a near-Jubilee redemption, though cheaper, is still a real payment. Luke 15:4–7: the shepherd leaves the ninety-nine to find the one lost sheep. No person is too small a redemption investment to be worth seeking. The Jubilee’s proportional pricing does not diminish the importance of any individual redemption.
53: Type: “He shall treat him as a worker hired year by year. He shall not rule over him ruthlessly (be-farekhk) in your sight.” Community accountability for how workers are treated mirrors the church’s corporate responsibility. Galatians 6:1–2: “Brothers, if anyone is caught in any transgression, you who are spiritual should restore him in a spirit of gentleness... Bear one another’s burdens, and so fulfill the law of Christ.” The community’s watchful care against “crushing labor” is fulfilled in the church’s burden-bearing.
54: Type: Even without a redeemer, the Jubilee provides automatic release. The guaranteed freedom regardless of external conditions is the strongest anti-slavery provision in the Levitical law. Romans 8:1: “There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus.” The believer’s freedom in Christ is as automatic and unconditional as the Jubilee release: not dependent on human intermediary or favorable circumstance but secured by divine appointment.
55: Direct type: “For it is to me that the people of Israel are servants. They are my servants whom I brought out of the land of Egypt: I am YHWH your God.” The chapter closes where it began: YHWH’s ownership claim grounded in the Exodus. 1 Corinthians 6:19–20: “you are not your own, for you were bought with a price. So glorify God in your body.” The new covenant Exodus accomplished by Christ creates the same identity: not your own, belonging wholly to the one who redeemed you. The Jubilee’s final word is the gospel’s first word: you have been bought — therefore live as the free servants of the one who bought you.
